source: TBPN — SpaceX IPO, token path, sovereign AI won't reach frontier
summary: OpenAI cutting Codex pricing signals the endgame is approaching; coding tokens are so valuable that labs must acquire them at scale to maintain RSI feedback loops
summary_extended: Baker argues that OpenAI's decision to aggressively cut Codex pricing is not a conventional competitive move but a strategic land-grab for coding tokens at scale, which he views as critical inputs to recursive self-improvement (RSI) feedback loops. His reasoning is that coding tokens occupy a uniquely privileged role in AI self-improvement cycles — labs that fail to accumulate sufficient coding token volume may be structurally excluded from entering RSI loops altogether, creating a winner-take-most dynamic. Baker interprets the urgency behind this pricing behavior as a signal that frontier AI labs believe the endgame of AI development is approaching imminently, with token acquisition now functioning as a form of competitive moat-building. The investment implication is directional rather than stock-specific: the race for coding tokens at scale favors the largest frontier labs with the resources to subsidize token acquisition, but since the primary actors (OpenAI, Anthropic) are private, there is no direct public equity expression of this thesis. Baker frames this as inference rather than confirmed fact, using 'I think' framing throughout.
detail: Baker interprets OpenAI's Codex price cuts as a strategic move to acquire coding tokens at scale, because coding tokens are critical inputs to recursive self-improvement (RSI) loops. If you don't have enough coding tokens, you may not be able to enter the RSI loop. This is why everyone is behaving like the endgame is near.
confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as his interpretation/inference rather than stated fact; uses 'I think' framing
quote_fragment: coding tokens are so valuable and if you don't have enough you may not get into this RSI loop
